<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the State Equation — Structured Logic Breakdown

The state equation $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x} + B\mathbf{u}$ is essentially a **compact, first-order, vector-matrix reformulation** of an $n$-th order linear differential equation. KTU examiners expect you to understand *why* this is the case.

- **Step 1 — Unfolding the $n$-th order ODE:** A general $n$-th order linear ODE with input $u(t)$ is:

$$
\frac{d^n y}{dt^n} + a_{n-1}\frac{d^{n-1} y}{dt^{n-1}} + \dots + a_1 \frac{dy}{dt} + a_0 y = b_0 u(t)
$$

- **Step 2 — Introducing the state variables:** We pick a *minimal* chain of derivatives as state variables. The most common choice (used in the **Phase-Variable Canonical Form**) is:

$$
x_1 = y, \quad x_2 = \dot{y}, \quad x_3 = \ddot{y}, \quad \dots, \quad x_n = y^{(n-1)}
$$

- **Step 3 — Forming first-order ODEs:** Differentiating once more, we get a system of $n$ first-order equations that is *exactly* the vector equation $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x} + B\mathbf{u}$.

- **Step 4 — Outputs:** If the measured output is $y(t) = x_1(t)$ (no derivatives of input), then $C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & \dots & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ and $D = 0$.

## 2.2 Why State-Space? The Real Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Robotics (PCRUT601 context):**
> 1. **Multi-Input Multi-Output (MIMO) ready:** A 6-DOF robotic arm has 6 inputs and 6 outputs. Classical transfer functions (one-input-one-output) cannot handle this. State-space handles MIMO *natively*.
> 2. **Time-domain insight:** Stability is read directly from eigenvalues of $A$. Controllability / Observability are matrix rank tests — vital for sensor/actuator placement in robots.
> 3. **Modern controller design:** LQR, pole-placement, Kalman filtering, and Model Predictive Control are **all** formulated in state-space.
> 4. **Initial conditions are explicit:** Unlike transfer functions, state-space carries the *initial state* $\mathbf{x}(0)$ as a first-class citizen — crucial when restarting a robot after an emergency stop.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Dimensions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | State equation (LTI, continuous) | $\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = A\mathbf{x}(t) + B\mathbf{u}(t)$ | $A: n \times n$, $B: n \times p$ |
| 2 | Output equation | $\mathbf{y}(t) = C\mathbf{x}(t) + D\mathbf{u}(t)$ | $C: q \times n$, $D: q \times p$ |
| 3 | State vector | $\mathbf{x}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 & x_2 & \dots & x_n \end{bmatrix}^T$ | $n$ = order of the system |
| 4 | Eigenvalues (open-loop poles) | $\det(\lambda I - A) = 0$ | Stability: $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$ |
| 5 | Characteristic polynomial | $q(\lambda) = \lambda^n + a_{n-1}\lambda^{n-1} + \dots + a_0$ | Coefficients match $A$'s trace / det |
| 6 | State transition matrix | $\Phi(t) = e^{At} = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\}$ | $\Phi(0) = I$ |
| 7 | Solution (zero input) | $\mathbf{x}(t) = \Phi(t)\,\mathbf{x}(0)$ | Homogeneous part |
| 8 | Solution (general) | $\mathbf{x}(t) = \Phi(t)\mathbf{x}(0) + \int_0^t \Phi(t-\tau)\,B\,\mathbf{u}(\tau)\,d\tau$ | Convolution form |
| 9 | Transfer function from SS | $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B + D$ | $q \times p$ matrix |
| 10 | Phase-variable $A$ matrix | $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & & & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \\ -a_0 & -a_1 & -a_2 & \cdots & -a_{n-1} \end{bmatrix}$ | Companion form |
| 11 | Phase-variable $B$ matrix | $B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $n \times 1$ |
| 12 | Output matrix (phase-var.) | $C = \begin{bmatrix} b_0 & b_1 & \dots & b_{n-1} \end{bmatrix}$ | $1 \times n$ |
| 13 | Phase-variable $D$ | $D = 0$ for strictly proper systems | $1 \times 1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TRICK — Pipe Character in LaTeX:**
> When writing $|sI - A|$ or absolute-value conditions inside any markdown table row, ALWAYS write $\lvert sI - A \rvert$ or $\vert sI - A \vert$. The vertical bar `\vert` inside a normal `|` table-cell pipe will *break* the markdown table and your answer sheet will render as garbage in the PDF.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From $n$-th Order ODE to State-Space (Phase-Variable Form)

This is the **single most frequently tested derivation** in KTU Module 4. We will derive it with **every** algebraic step explicit.

**Given** a single-input, single-output (SISO) linear time-invariant system described by the $n$-th order ODE:

$$
\frac{d^n y(t)}{dt^n} + a_{n-1}\frac{d^{n-1} y(t)}{dt^{n-1}} + a_{n-2}\frac{d^{n-2} y(t)}{dt^{n-2}} + \dots + a_1 \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + a_0 y(t) = b_0\, u(t)
$$

**Goal:** Convert this into the state-space form $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x} + B u$, $\quad y = C\mathbf{x} + D u$.

**Step 1 — Define the state variables** (this is the *definition* part, not a derived result):

$$
x_1(t) = y(t)
$$

$$
x_2(t) = \dot{y}(t) = \dot{x}_1(t)
$$

$$
x_3(t) = \ddot{y}(t) = \dot{x}_2(t)
$$

$$
\vdots
$$

$$
x_n(t) = \frac{d^{n-1} y(t)}{dt^{n-1}} = \dot{x}_{n-1}(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Form the first $n-1$ state equations** (these come directly from the definitions above):

$$
\dot{x}_1(t) = x_2(t)
$$

$$
\dot{x}_2(t) = x_3(t)
$$

$$
\dot{x}_3(t) = x_4(t)
$$

$$
\vdots
$$

$$
\dot{x}_{n-1}(t) = x_n(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Form the $n$-th state equation** (this is the only non-trivial one — it uses the original ODE):

We have, by definition of the $n$-th state:

$$
\dot{x}_n(t) = \frac{d^n y(t)}{dt^n}
$$

From the original ODE, we solve for the highest derivative:

$$
\frac{d^n y}{dt^n} = -a_0 y - a_1 \dot{y} - a_2 \ddot{y} - \dots - a_{n-1}\frac{d^{n-1}y}{dt^{n-1}} + b_0 u
$$

Substituting $x_1 = y$, $x_2 = \dot{y}$, $\dots$, $x_n = y^{(n-1)}$:

$$
\dot{x}_n(t) = -a_0 x_1(t) - a_1 x_2(t) - a_2 x_3(t) - \dots - a_{n-1} x_n(t) + b_0 u(t)
$$

**Step 4 — Assemble into matrix form** $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x} + B u$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \\ \dot{x}_3 \\ \vdots \\ \dot{x}_{n-1} \\ \dot{x}_n \end{bmatrix} &=
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 1 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \\
-a_0 & -a_1 & -a_2 & -a_3 & \cdots & -a_{n-1}
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \\ \vdots \\ x_{n-1} \\ x_n \end{bmatrix}
+
\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \\ b_0 \end{bmatrix} u(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Output equation** (since the output is $y = x_1$):

$$
y(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ \vdots \\ x_n \end{bmatrix} + (0)\,u(t)
$$

Therefore $C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ and $D = 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Companion Matrix" $A$ is the takeaway.** Notice how the coefficients $\{a_0, a_1, \dots, a_{n-1}\}$ from the original ODE appear (with a **sign flip**) in the *last row* of the $A$ matrix. This is the most heavily tested fact in the KTU 2024 Module 4 exam.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — 2nd Order System (KTU-favorite)

**Problem:** Convert the following mechanical/electrical system equation into state-space:

$$
\ddot{y}(t) + 6\dot{y}(t) + 11 y(t) = 5 u(t)
$$

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify coefficients:** $a_0 = 11$, $a_1 = 6$, $b_0 = 5$, $n = 2$.

**Step 2 — Choose state variables:**

$$
x_1 = y, \quad x_2 = \dot{y}
$$

**Step 3 — Form state equations:**

$$
\dot{x}_1 = x_2
$$

$$
\dot{x}_2 = \ddot{y} = -11 y - 6 \dot{y} + 5 u = -11 x_1 - 6 x_2 + 5 u
$$

**Step 4 — Write in matrix form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} &=
\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}
+
\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix} u(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
y(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify eigenvalues (open-loop poles):**

$$
\det(\lambda I - A) = \det \begin{bmatrix} \lambda & -1 \\ 11 & \lambda + 6 \end{bmatrix} = \lambda(\lambda + 6) + 11 = \lambda^2 + 6\lambda + 11
$$

Setting this to zero: $\lambda^2 + 6\lambda + 11 = 0 \implies \lambda = -3 \pm j\sqrt{2}$. These are exactly the poles of the original ODE — confirming the conversion is correct.

## 3.3 Worked Example — General 3rd Order System (Full Mastery)

**Given ODE:**

$$
\dddot{y} + 8\ddot{y} + 19\dot{y} + 12 y = 6 u
$$

**Step 1 — Coefficients:** $n=3$, $a_0 = 12$, $a_1 = 19$, $a_2 = 8$, $b_0 = 6$.

**Step 2 — States:** $x_1 = y$, $x_2 = \dot{y}$, $x_3 = \ddot{y}$.

**Step 3 — State equations:**

$$
\dot{x}_1 = x_2
$$

$$
\dot{x}_2 = x_3
$$

$$
\dot{x}_3 = -12 x_1 - 19 x_2 - 8 x_3 + 6 u
$$

**Step 4 — Matrix form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -12 & -19 & -8 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
D = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Solution of the State Equation (Time-Domain)

The state equation $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x} + B\mathbf{u}$ is a first-order linear ODE in vector form. Its **complete analytical solution** for time $t \geq 0$ is:

$$
\mathbf{x}(t) = \underbrace{e^{At}\,\mathbf{x}(0)}_{\text{Zero-input response (free response)}} + \underbrace{\int_0^{t} e^{A(t-\tau)}\,B\,\mathbf{u}(\tau)\,d\tau}_{\text{Zero-state response (forced response)}}
$$

The matrix exponential $e^{At}$ is called the **state transition matrix** $\Phi(t)$ and is computed as:

$$
\Phi(t) = e^{At} = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{ (sI - A)^{-1} \right\} = I + At + \frac{(At)^2}{2!} + \frac{(At)^3}{3!} + \dots
$$

Key properties (commonly asked as 3-mark questions):

1. $\Phi(0) = I$ (the identity matrix)
2. $\Phi^{-1}(t) = \Phi(-t)$
3. $\Phi(t_1 + t_2) = \Phi(t_1)\,\Phi(t_2)$
4. $\dot{\Phi}(t) = A\,\Phi(t) = \Phi(t)\,A$

## 3.5 Transfer Function from State-Space

Taking the Laplace transform of the state equations with zero initial conditions:

$$
s\,\mathbf{X}(s) = A\,\mathbf{X}(s) + B\,\mathbf{U}(s) \implies \mathbf{X}(s) = (sI - A)^{-1} B\,\mathbf{U}(s)
$$

$$
\mathbf{Y}(s) = C\,\mathbf{X}(s) + D\,\mathbf{U}(s)
$$

Substituting:

$$
\mathbf{Y}(s) = \left[ C(sI - A)^{-1} B + D \right] \mathbf{U}(s)
$$

Therefore the **transfer function matrix** is:

$$
G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1} B + D
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** For a SISO system, $G(s)$ becomes a scalar. The denominator of $G(s)$ equals $\det(sI - A)$ (the characteristic polynomial), and the numerator is $C\,\text{adj}(sI - A)\,B + D\det(sI - A)$. This is the bridge between classical control and modern (state-space) control.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — State-Signal Flow Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **functional architecture** of a state-space system — the flow of signals between state, input, and output:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U["Input u(t)"] --> SUM[("Summing Junction")]
    B["Input Matrix B"] --> SUM
    SUM --> INTEG1["dx1/dt = x2"]
    SUM --> INTEG2["dx2/dt = x3"]
    SUM --> INTEGN["dxn/dt = -a0 x1 - a1 x2 ... - an-1 xn + b0 u"]
    INTEG1 --> X1["State x1 = y"]
    INTEG2 --> X2["State x2"]
    INTEGN --> XN["State xn"]
    A["System Matrix A"] -. feedback coupling .-> SUM
    X1 --> Y["Output y(t)"]
    X2 --> Y
    XN --> Y
    C["Output Matrix C"] -. output mapping .-> Y
    D["Feedthrough D"] --> Y
    X1 -.-> A
    X2 -.-> A
    XN -.-> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The input $u(t)$ is multiplied by $B$, added to the state feedback $A\mathbf{x}$, and the result is integrated (the $\int$ blocks) to produce the new state. The output matrix $C$ then selects which states become the measured outputs. The dotted lines from each $x_i$ into block $A$ indicate that **all** states couple into the dynamics matrix.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — The $n$-th Order ODE to State-Space Conversion Process

This Mermaid diagram shows the *methodological* workflow a KTU student should follow when given any linear ODE:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Step 1: Read the nth order linear ODE"] --> P2["Step 2: Identify coefficients a0 ... an-1 and b0"]
    P2 --> P3["Step 3: Choose state variables x1 = y, x2 = y-dot, ..., xn = y to the power n minus 1"]
    P3 --> P4["Step 4: Write first n-1 state equations as trivial chain x-dot-i = x-sub-i+1"]
    P4 --> P5["Step 5: Form the nth equation using the ODE: x-dot-n = negative sum of a-sub-k times x-sub-k plus b0 u"]
    P5 --> P6["Step 6: Assemble A as companion matrix, B as column with b0 at bottom, C as 1 0 ... 0, D equals 0"]
    P6 --> P7["Step 7: Verify by checking det lambda I minus A equals characteristic polynomial"]
    P7 --> OUT["Final State-Space Form: x-dot equals A x plus B u, y equals C x plus D u"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Eigenvalue-Pole Correspondence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ODE["nth order ODE"] -- "Coefficients a0 ... an-1" --> CP["Characteristic Polynomial: s to the n plus a-sub-n-1 s to the n-1 ... plus a0"]
    CP -- "Set to zero, find roots" --> POLES["Open-loop poles p1 p2 ... pn"]
    AMAT["System Matrix A"] -- "Compute det lambda I minus A equals 0" --> EIGS["Eigenvalues lambda 1 ... lambda n"]
    POLES == "MUST MATCH" ==> EIGS
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Validation Rule:** The roots of the characteristic polynomial derived from the ODE must *exactly equal* the eigenvalues of the $A$ matrix. This is the single most reliable self-check in the examination hall. If they don't match, you have made a sign error in the last row of $A$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the terms (i) state, (ii) state variable, and (iii) state vector of a dynamic system. What is the minimum number of state variables required for an $n$-th order system?**

**Model Answer:**

- **(i) State:** The state of a dynamic system at time $t = t_0$ is the *minimum* amount of information (i.e., the smallest set of numbers) that, together with the input for $t \geq t_0$, completely determines the future behavior of the system for $t \geq t_0$.
- **(ii) State Variable:** A state variable is any one of the $n$ independent variables that constitute the state of the system. They must be linearly independent, and no state variable can be written as a linear combination of the others.
- **(iii) State Vector:** It is the column vector formed by stacking all $n$ state variables. If $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$ are the state variables, the state vector is $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T$.

**Minimum number of state variables = $n$** (the order of the governing differential equation). For example, a 2nd order system requires exactly 2 state variables.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Write the general state-space representation of a linear time-invariant continuous-time system in matrix form and identify each matrix with its physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**

The standard form is:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = A\,\mathbf{x}(t) + B\,\mathbf{u}(t), \qquad \mathbf{y}(t) = C\,\mathbf{x}(t) + D\,\mathbf{u}(t)
$$

| Matrix | Symbol | Physical Significance |
|---|---|---|
| State matrix | $A \; (n \times n)$ | Describes the *internal dynamics* — how states evolve without external input. The eigenvalues of $A$ are the open-loop poles. |
| Input matrix | $B \; (n \times p)$ | Describes *how each input* is coupled into the state derivatives. |
| Output matrix | $C \; (q \times n)$ | Defines *which linear combination* of states is measured as output. |
| Feed-through matrix | $D \; (q \times p)$ | Represents the *direct* (instantaneous) transmission from input to output, bypassing the dynamics. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern, Module 4 questions of 14 marks are offered as **internal choice** — the student answers *either* Question A *or* Question B. Both are provided below with full worked solutions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]**

**(a)** Derive the state-space representation in **phase-variable canonical form** for the following linear differential equation:

$$
\frac{d^3 y(t)}{dt^3} + 9 \frac{d^2 y(t)}{dt^2} + 23 \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + 15 y(t) = 4 u(t)
$$

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the resulting state-space system, determine:
1. The characteristic polynomial and the open-loop poles.
2. The transfer function $G(s) = Y(s)/U(s)$ using $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B + D$.

**(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters:** $n = 3$, $a_0 = 15$, $a_1 = 23$, $a_2 = 9$, $b_0 = 4$.

**Step 2 — Define the state variables:**

$$
x_1 = y, \quad x_2 = \dot{y}, \quad x_3 = \ddot{y}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the first $n-1 = 2$ state equations** (trivially, by definition):

$$
\dot{x}_1 = x_2, \qquad \dot{x}_2 = x_3
$$

**Step 4 — Form the third state equation** by isolating the highest derivative in the ODE:

$$
\dddot{y} = -15 y - 23 \dot{y} - 9 \ddot{y} + 4 u
$$

Substituting $x_1, x_2, x_3$:

$$
\dot{x}_3 = -15 x_1 - 23 x_2 - 9 x_3 + 4 u
$$

**Step 5 — Assemble matrices** (last row of $A$ has coefficients with a sign flip):

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -15 & -23 & -9 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
D = 0
$$

**Part (b):**

**1. Characteristic polynomial:**

$$
\det(sI - A) = \det \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & s & -1 \\ 15 & 23 & s+9 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Expanding along the first row:

$$
\det(sI - A) = s \cdot \det \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 23 & s+9 \end{bmatrix} - (-1) \cdot \det \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 15 & s+9 \end{bmatrix} + 0
$$

$$
= s[s(s+9) - (-1)(23)] + [0 \cdot (s+9) - (-1)(15)]
$$

$$
= s(s^2 + 9s + 23) + 15 = s^3 + 9s^2 + 23s + 15
$$

Setting this to zero: $s^3 + 9s^2 + 23s + 15 = 0$.

**Finding the poles** — try $s = -1$:

$$
(-1)^3 + 9(-1)^2 + 23(-1) + 15 = -1 + 9 - 23 + 15 = 0 \;\; \checkmark
$$

Factor out $(s+1)$:

$$
s^3 + 9s^2 + 23s + 15 = (s+1)(s^2 + 8s + 15) = (s+1)(s+3)(s+5)
$$

**Open-loop poles:** $s = -1, \; -3, \; -5$. All in the LHP $\Rightarrow$ **system is stable**.

**2. Transfer function:**

We need $(sI - A)^{-1}$. Using the adjugate formula, or simply using the fact that for phase-variable form:

$$
G(s) = \frac{b_0}{s^n + a_{n-1} s^{n-1} + \dots + a_0}
$$

(with $D = 0$ for strictly-proper systems). Substituting:

$$
G(s) = \frac{4}{s^3 + 9s^2 + 23s + 15} = \frac{4}{(s+1)(s+3)(s+5)}
$$

We can verify with the full matrix expression:

$$
(sI - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{s^3+9s^2+23s+15}\begin{bmatrix} s^2+9s+23 & s+9 & 1 \\ -15 & s(s+9) & s \\ 15s & 23s+15 & s^2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

(We omit the full adjugate expansion for brevity, but the multiplication $C(sI-A)^{-1}B$ recovers the numerator $4$.)

**Final Answer (Q-A):**

$$
\boxed{\; A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -15 & -23 & -9 \end{bmatrix}, \;
B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix}, \;
C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \;
D = 0 \;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; G(s) = \frac{4}{(s+1)(s+3)(s+5)} \;}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Model]**

**(a)** A robotic joint's servo motor is modeled by the following transfer function:

$$
G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{2s + 5}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6}
$$

**Obtain the controllable canonical form (CCF) state-space representation of this system.**

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same system, find:
1. The state transition matrix $\Phi(s) = (sI - A)^{-1}$ in matrix form.
2. The poles of the system, and comment on stability.

**(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

The given transfer function is of the form:

$$
G(s) = \frac{b_2 s^2 + b_1 s + b_0}{s^3 + a_2 s^2 + a_1 s + a_0}
$$

where $a_0 = 6$, $a_1 = 11$, $a_2 = 6$, and $b_0 = 5$, $b_1 = 2$, $b_2 = 0$.

(Note: the numerator degree 2 < denominator degree 3, so the system is *strictly proper* and $D = 0$.)

**Controllable Canonical Form matrices** (note the *sign convention* used in KTU textbooks — the last row of $A$ has the *negated* coefficients):

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -a_0 & -a_1 & -a_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
C = \begin{bmatrix} b_0 & b_1 & b_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
D = 0
$$

**Verification of transfer function:**

$$
G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1} B = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \frac{1}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ s \\ s^2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{5 + 2s + 0}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6}
$$

which exactly matches the given $G(s)$. ✓

**Part (b):**

**1. Computing $(sI - A)^{-1}$:**

$$
sI - A = \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & s & -1 \\ 6 & 11 & s+6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The determinant of $(sI - A)$ (which is the characteristic polynomial) is:

$$
\Delta(s) = s \cdot [s(s+6) + 11] - (-1)[0 \cdot (s+6) + 6] + 0
$$

$$
= s(s^2 + 6s + 11) + 6 = s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6
$$

**Adjugate matrix** (transpose of cofactor matrix):

$$
\text{adj}(sI - A) = \begin{bmatrix}
s^2 + 6s + 11 & s + 6 & 1 \\
6 & s(s+6) & s \\
-6s & -11s - 6 & s^2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Therefore:

$$
(sI - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6} \begin{bmatrix}
s^2 + 6s + 11 & s + 6 & 1 \\
6 & s^2 + 6s & s \\
-6s & -11s - 6 & s^2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**2. Poles and stability:**

The poles are the roots of $s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6 = 0$.

Test $s = -1$:

$$
-1 + 6 - 11 + 6 = 0 \;\; \checkmark
$$

Factor: $s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6 = (s+1)(s^2 + 5s + 6) = (s+1)(s+2)(s+3)$.

**Poles:** $s = -1, \; -2, \; -3$. All three lie in the **left half-plane (LHP)**, hence the system is **BIBO stable** and **asymptotically stable**.

**Final Answer (Q-B):**

$$
\boxed{\; A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix}, \;
B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \;
C = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \;
D = 0 \;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; \text{Poles} = \{-1, -2, -3\}, \quad \text{System is stable.} \;}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where You Will Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign flip confusion (most common error, –3 marks):** Students often write the last row of $A$ as $+a_0, +a_1, \dots$ instead of $-a_0, -a_1, \dots$. The ODE has $a_k$ on the LHS with a $+1$ on the $\dddot y$ term, so when you isolate $\dddot y$ everything on the RHS gets negated. **Always double-check the sign.**
> 2. **Forgetting to dimension $A$ as $n \times n$:** A 3rd order system must give a $3 \times 3$ $A$ matrix. If you write a $2 \times 2$, you have under-defined the state.
> 3. **Mixing up Controllable vs Observable canonical forms:** CCF has the *coefficients* in the last row of $A$ and the *numerator coefficients* in $C$. OCF has them in the *first column* of $A$ and a different $C$. KTU will mark you strictly on which form was asked.
> 4. **Pipe character in markdown tables:** If your answer script (especially digital submissions) uses `$|sI - A|$` inside a table row, the table breaks. Use `\lvert \rvert` or `\vert`.
> 5. **Not verifying the transfer function:** Always cross-check $G(s) = C(sI-A)^{-1}B$ against the original ODE/TF. If the two don't match, you've lost 1–2 marks minimum.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **State** = minimum information set + input → determines the entire future. **State variables** = the $n$ independent quantities. **State vector** $\mathbf{x}(t)$ = column vector of all $n$ state variables. **State space** = the $n$-dimensional space $\mathbf{x}$ moves through.
- The **canonical state equation** is **two** equations: $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x} + B\mathbf{u}$ (state equation) and $\mathbf{y} = C\mathbf{x} + D\mathbf{u}$ (output equation). Memorize the dimensions: $A: n \times n$, $B: n \times p$, $C: q \times n$, $D: q \times p$.
- For an $n$-th order ODE, the minimum number of state variables is exactly **$n$**. The phase-variable choice $x_1 = y, \; x_2 = \dot{y}, \; \dots, \; x_n = y^{(n-1)}$ is standard.
- The **last row of $A$** in phase-variable form contains the **negated ODE coefficients** in the order $[-a_0, -a_1, \dots, -a_{n-1}]$. This is the #1 KTU Module 4 fact.
- The column $B$ has $b_0$ in the **last** entry (or $1$ for controllable canonical form, depending on convention). Always specify which canonical form you are using.
- The output matrix $C = [1 \; 0 \; \cdots \; 0]$ when the output is the first state variable. $D = 0$ for strictly proper systems.
- The **state transition matrix** $\Phi(t) = e^{At}$ is computed via Laplace: $\Phi(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI-A)^{-1}\}$. Properties: $\Phi(0) = I$, $\Phi(t_1+t_2) = \Phi(t_1)\Phi(t_2)$, $\Phi^{-1}(t) = \Phi(-t)$.
- The **general solution** splits into **zero-input response** (homogeneous, depends on $\mathbf{x}(0)$) and **zero-state response** (forced, convolution with input via $B$).
- The **transfer function** from state-space: $G(s) = C(sI-A)^{-1}B + D$. The denominator equals $\det(sI - A)$, which is the **characteristic polynomial**.
- The poles of the system are the **eigenvalues of $A$**, found from $\det(\lambda I - A) = 0$. Stability criterion: $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$ for all $i$.
- Canonical forms: **Phase-variable / Companion (CCF)** — coefficients in last row of $A$; **Observable (OCF)** — coefficients in first column of $A$; **Diagonal / Jordan** — when $A$ is diagonalized (modal form).
- **For robotic systems (PCRUT601 context):** Always use state-space for multi-DOF arms. The $A$ matrix will encode joint coupling, $B$ will contain motor torque inputs, $C$ selects encoder-measured positions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Matrix Representation of State Equations. Phase Variable and Canonical Forms of State Representation - Controllable
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT601 - ROBOTIC CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Matrix Representation of State Equations. Phase Variable and Canonical Forms of State Representation - Controllable

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **State Space Representation** is a modern, time-domain mathematical framework used to model, analyze, and design control systems using a set of first-order differential equations expressed in matrix form. For a linear, time-invariant (LTI), continuous-time system, the standard matrix representation of the state equation and the output equation is given by:

The **State Equation** describes how the internal energy-storing elements (states) evolve with time:
$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}(t)
$$

The **Output Equation** describes how the measurable outputs relate to the states and inputs:
$$
\mathbf{y}(t) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{D}\mathbf{u}(t)
$$

where the system matrices have the following dimensional conventions for an $n^{th}$ order system with $r$ inputs and $m$ outputs:
- $\mathbf{x}(t)$ is the $n \times 1$ state vector
- $\mathbf{u}(t)$ is the $r \times 1$ input vector
- $\mathbf{y}(t)$ is the $m \times 1$ output vector
- $\mathbf{A}$ is the $n \times n$ system matrix (state matrix)
- $\mathbf{B}$ is the $n \times r$ input matrix
- $\mathbf{C}$ is the $m \times n$ output matrix
- $\mathbf{D}$ is the $m \times r$ direct transmission (feedforward) matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 emphasizes the structural forms of state representation. The student is expected to derive the **Phase Variable Form (PVF)** and the **Controllable Canonical Form (CCF)** directly from the transfer function of a Single-Input Single-Output (SISO) system.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Car Dashboard Analogy

Imagine you are driving a car (the *plant* / system). You control only two things from the driver's seat: the accelerator pedal and the steering wheel (these are your *inputs* $\mathbf{u}$). You can directly *measure* the speed of the car from the speedometer (the *output* $\mathbf{y}$).

But the car has many hidden internal variables — the engine RPM, the fuel-air mixture ratio, the wheel angular velocity, the temperature of the coolant — which you cannot see directly from the dashboard. These hidden variables are the **states** $\mathbf{x}$. They completely describe the past, present, and future behavior of the car.

**Matrix representation** is essentially a compact way of writing down all the "hidden" differential equations at once. Instead of writing $n$ long scalar equations, we bundle them into one elegant matrix equation $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}$.

**Phase Variable Form** is like reorganizing the dashboard so the variables are displayed in a specific logical order (e.g., position, then velocity, then acceleration) — a "cascaded" view of the system's internal workings.

**Controllable Canonical Form** is a special structural arrangement where the matrices $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{B}$ have a very specific pattern that guarantees the system is *controllable* — meaning you can move the car from *any* starting speed to *any* desired speed using only the accelerator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** State space representation works for **Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (MIMO)** systems, unlike the classical transfer function approach which is limited to SISO systems. This makes it indispensable for modern robotic control.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following conventions are universally adopted in the KTU 2024 scheme for state representation:
- The system order $n$ is denoted by the highest power of $s$ in the characteristic polynomial.
- **Coefficient sign convention:** The characteristic polynomial is always written in **monic form** as $s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + a_2 s^{n-2} + \cdots + a_{n-1}s + a_n$. The coefficient of $s^n$ is **normalized to 1**.
- All state variables in canonical forms are dimensionless abstractions of physical quantities (e.g., position $\rightarrow$ rad, velocity $\rightarrow$ rad/s).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Decomposition — The Three Pillars of State Representation

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically targets three structural arrangements of the system matrices $\mathbf{A}$, $\mathbf{B}$, $\mathbf{C}$, and $\mathbf{D}$. Each has a specific "shape" or **canonical structure**.

#### Pillar 1: The General State Space Form
Any $n^{th}$ order linear differential equation can be converted into $n$ first-order differential equations. The matrices $\mathbf{A}$, $\mathbf{B}$, $\mathbf{C}$, $\mathbf{D}$ can take **any arbitrary form** depending on how the states are chosen.

#### Pillar 2: The Phase Variable Form (PVF)
The **Phase Variables** are a specific set of state variables chosen as the output of cascaded integrators when a single-input signal propagates through the system. The state variables are defined as:

$$
x_1 = y, \quad x_2 = \dot{y}, \quad x_3 = \ddot{y}, \quad \ldots, \quad x_n = y^{(n-1)}
$$

The phase variable form has a characteristic **companion matrix structure** for $\mathbf{A}$ where the upper sub-diagonal contains 1s, and the last row contains the negative of the characteristic polynomial coefficients.

#### Pillar 3: The Controllable Canonical Form (CCF)
The Controllable Canonical Form is a special case of the Phase Variable Form where the structure of $\mathbf{B}$ is uniquely fixed to $\mathbf{B} = [0, 0, \ldots, 0, 1]^T$. The Controllability matrix $\mathcal{C} = [\mathbf{B} \; \mathbf{AB} \; \mathbf{A}^2\mathbf{B} \; \ldots \; \mathbf{A}^{n-1}\mathbf{B}]$ is always guaranteed to be full rank $n$ for this form — hence the name "controllable canonical."

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The table below summarizes the **canonical structures** that KTU examiners expect students to reproduce from memory. The convention is a monic denominator $s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_n$ and a numerator $b_0 s^n + b_1 s^{n-1} + \cdots + b_n$.

| Property | Phase Variable Form (PVF) | Controllable Canonical Form (CCF) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Input Matrix $\mathbf{B}$** | Arbitrary (depends on input coefficients) | $\mathbf{B} = [0, \; 0, \; \ldots, \; 0, \; 1]^T$ (unique) |
| **System Matrix $\mathbf{A}$ Shape** | Companion matrix with $-a_n, -a_{n-1}, \ldots, -a_1$ in last row | Same companion structure as PVF |
| **Output Matrix $\mathbf{C}$** | Row of numerator coefficients $[b_n, \; b_{n-1}, \; \ldots, \; b_1]$ | Row of *modified* coefficients $[b_n - a_n b_0, \; \ldots, \; b_0]$ |
| **Feedforward $\mathbf{D}$** | Typically 0 for strictly proper systems | $b_0$ (for proper transfer functions) |
| **Controllability Guarantee** | Not guaranteed | Always guaranteed (by construction) |
| **Relationship** | General companion structure | Special constrained case of PVF |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** The most heavily tested topic in Module 4 is the conversion from a transfer function $G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)}$ directly into the Controllable Canonical Form. Memorize the exact positions of $-a_i$ in the last row of $\mathbf{A}$ and the placement of $b_j$ in $\mathbf{C}$.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Controllable Canonical Form is not merely a mathematical curiosity — it has critical practical applications in modern robotics and control engineering:

1. **Ackermann's Formula for Pole Placement:** When designing a state-feedback controller $\mathbf{u} = -\mathbf{K}\mathbf{x}$ to place the closed-loop poles at desired locations, the **Ackermann's formula** is derived using the controllable canonical form. Without the CCF structure, the formula becomes computationally intractable.

2. **Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR) Design:** The LQR design procedure requires solving the Algebraic Riccati Equation (ARE), and the iterative solution converges much faster when the system is in CCF.

3. **Model Predictive Control (MPC):** Modern robotic manipulators and autonomous vehicles use MPC, which internally maintains the system in a canonical form for rapid re-linearization at every sampling instant.

4. **Digital Twin and Observer Design:** When designing a Luenberger observer or a Kalman filter, the CCF simplifies the derivation of the observer gain matrix $\mathbf{L}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The term "canonical" in control theory has a precise meaning: it refers to a representation that is *unique* (up to similarity transformations) and *structurally minimal*. The CCF is canonical because the system matrices are uniquely determined by the transfer function coefficients $a_i$ and $b_j$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Transfer Function to Controllable Canonical Form

We are given the general SISO transfer function of an $n^{th}$ order system:

$$
G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{b_0 s^n + b_1 s^{n-1} + b_2 s^{n-2} + \cdots + b_{n-1} s + b_n}{s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + a_2 s^{n-2} + \cdots + a_{n-1} s + a_n}
$$

**Step 1 — Cross-multiply to obtain the input-output differential equation.**

Multiplying both sides by the denominator and applying the inverse Laplace transform (assuming zero initial conditions for the transfer function definition):

$$
\begin{aligned}
(s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_n) Y(s) &= (b_0 s^n + b_1 s^{n-1} + \cdots + b_n) U(s)
\end{aligned}
$$

In the time domain, the operator $s$ corresponds to differentiation $\frac{d}{dt}$. Thus:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y^{(n)} + a_1 y^{(n-1)} + a_2 y^{(n-2)} + \cdots + a_n y &= b_0 u^{(n)} + b_1 u^{(n-1)} + \cdots + b_n u
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the phase variables by introducing an intermediate variable $X(s)$.**

To remove the direct feedthrough (the $b_0$ term which complicates the state equations), we introduce an auxiliary variable:

$$
X(s) = \frac{1}{s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_n} \cdot U(s)
$$

This means $X(s)$ satisfies the strictly proper part of the transfer function. Equivalently:

$$
x^{(n)} + a_1 x^{(n-1)} + a_2 x^{(n-2)} + \cdots + a_n x = u
$$

**Step 3 — Choose the state variables as the output of cascaded integrators.**

This is the defining step for the phase variable / controllable canonical form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= x \\
x_2 &= \dot{x} \\
x_3 &= \ddot{x} \\
&\vdots \\
x_n &= x^{(n-1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Write the first-order differential equations for each state.**

Differentiating each definition, we obtain a chain of $n$ first-order equations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}_1 &= x_2 \\
\dot{x}_2 &= x_3 \\
\dot{x}_3 &= x_4 \\
&\vdots \\
\dot{x}_{n-1} &= x_n \\
\dot{x}_n &= x^{(n)} = -a_n x_1 - a_{n-1} x_2 - \cdots - a_1 x_n + u
\end{aligned}
$$

The last equation uses the strictly proper relation from Step 2, which gives $x^{(n)} = -a_1 x^{(n-1)} - a_2 x^{(n-2)} - \cdots - a_n x + u$.

**Step 5 — Write the system in matrix form $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}$.**

Stacking the $n$ first-order equations into a single vector-matrix equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \\ \dot{x}_3 \\ \vdots \\ \dot{x}_{n-1} \\ \dot{x}_n \end{bmatrix} =
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 1 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \\
-a_n & -a_{n-1} & -a_{n-2} & \cdots & -a_2 & -a_1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \\ \vdots \\ x_{n-1} \\ x_n \end{bmatrix} +
\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u
\end{aligned}
$$

This yields the **canonical system matrix** $\mathbf{A}$ and **canonical input matrix** $\mathbf{B}$:

$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \\
-a_n & -a_{n-1} & -a_{n-2} & \cdots & -a_1
\end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Derive the output equation $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{D}\mathbf{u}$.**

Since $Y(s) = (b_0 s^n + b_1 s^{n-1} + \cdots + b_n) X(s)$, and the numerator can be split as $b_0 s^n X(s) + (b_1 s^{n-1} + \cdots + b_n) X(s)$, we substitute the strictly proper differential relation to eliminate the highest derivative:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y &= b_0 \bigl(-a_1 x_n - a_2 x_{n-1} - \cdots - a_n x_1 + u\bigr) + b_1 x_n + b_2 x_{n-1} + \cdots + b_n x_1
\end{aligned}
$$

Grouping terms by the state variables:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y &= (b_n - a_n b_0) x_1 + (b_{n-1} - a_{n-1} b_0) x_2 + \cdots + (b_1 - a_1 b_0) x_n + b_0 u
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the output matrix and feedforward matrix are:

$$
\mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} b_n - a_n b_0 & b_{n-1} - a_{n-1} b_0 & \cdots & b_1 - a_1 b_0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{D} = b_0
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example — A Third-Order System (KTU Standard Problem Pattern)

**Problem:** Convert the following transfer function into Controllable Canonical Form:

$$
G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{2s^3 + 5s^2 + 3s + 7}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 4}
$$

**Solution — Step 1: Identify coefficients.**

Comparing with the standard form $G(s) = \frac{b_0 s^3 + b_1 s^2 + b_2 s + b_3}{s^3 + a_1 s^2 + a_2 s + a_3}$:

The denominator coefficients are: $a_1 = 6$, $a_2 = 11$, $a_3 = 4$.
The numerator coefficients are: $b_0 = 2$, $b_1 = 5$, $b_2 = 3$, $b_3 = 7$.

**Solution — Step 2: Construct the system matrix $\mathbf{A}$ (a $3 \times 3$ companion matrix).**

Using the formula derived above with $n=3$:

$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1 \\
-a_3 & -a_2 & -a_1
\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1 \\
-4 & -11 & -6
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Solution — Step 3: Construct the input matrix $\mathbf{B}$.**

$$
\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Solution — Step 4: Construct the output matrix $\mathbf{C}$ using the modified coefficients.**

Computing each entry using the formula $C_i = b_{n+1-i} - a_{n+1-i} b_0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= b_3 - a_3 b_0 = 7 - (4)(2) = 7 - 8 = -1 \\
C_2 &= b_2 - a_2 b_0 = 3 - (11)(2) = 3 - 22 = -19 \\
C_3 &= b_1 - a_1 b_0 = 5 - (6)(2) = 5 - 12 = -7
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:
$$
\mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & -19 & -7 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Solution — Step 5: Identify the feedforward matrix $\mathbf{D}$.**

$$
\mathbf{D} = b_0 = 2
$$

**Solution — Step 6: Final verification using the characteristic polynomial.**

The characteristic polynomial of $\mathbf{A}$ should equal the denominator of $G(s)$. The characteristic polynomial is $\det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})$:

$$
\det\begin{bmatrix}
s & -1 & 0 \\
0 & s & -1 \\
4 & 11 & s+6
\end{bmatrix} = s\bigl(s(s+6) - (-1)(11)\bigr) - (-1)\bigl(0(s+6) - (-1)(4)\bigr) + 0
$$

Expanding:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= s(s^2 + 6s + 11) - 1(4) \\
&= s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s - 4
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — we need $s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 4$. The error is in the sign of the last term. Since the last row of $\mathbf{A}$ is $[-4, -11, -6]$, the determinant of $s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}$ gives the correct $+4$ because the bottom row becomes $[+4, +11, s+6]$:

$$
\det\begin{bmatrix}
s & -1 & 0 \\
0 & s & -1 \\
4 & 11 & s+6
\end{bmatrix} = s(s^2 + 6s + 11) + 4 = s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 4 \quad \checkmark
$$

This matches the denominator exactly. The CCF conversion is verified.

### 3.3 Comprehensive Summary of the Derivations

| Matrix | Formula in Controllable Canonical Form | Dimensions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathbf{A}$ | Last row: $[-a_n, -a_{n-1}, \ldots, -a_1]$; Upper superdiagonal: all 1s; All other entries: 0 | $n \times n$ |
| $\mathbf{B}$ | $\mathbf{B} = [0, 0, \ldots, 0, 1]^T$ | $n \times 1$ |
| $\mathbf{C}$ | $\mathbf{C} = [b_n - a_n b_0, \; b_{n-1} - a_{n-1} b_0, \; \ldots, \; b_1 - a_1 b_0]$ | $1 \times n$ |
| $\mathbf{D}$ | $\mathbf{D} = b_0$ | $1 \times 1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight for KTU Exam:** If the transfer function is *strictly proper* (i.e., the order of the numerator is strictly less than the order of the denominator), then $b_0 = 0$ and the formulas simplify dramatically: $\mathbf{D} = 0$ and $\mathbf{C}$ becomes the simple row of numerator coefficients $[b_n, b_{n-1}, \ldots, b_1]$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Controllable Canonical Form

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the cascaded integrator structure that physically realizes the Controllable Canonical Form. Each integrator produces one state variable, and the feedback paths from the states implement the coefficients $a_i$.

```mermaid
graph TD
    Input["Input u(t)"] --> SumNode["Summing Junction (+)"]
    SumNode --> Int1["Integrator 1/s"]
    Int1 --> State1["State x1"]
    State1 --> Int2["Integrator 1/s"]
    Int2 --> State2["State x2"]
    State2 --> Int3["Integrator 1/s"]
    Int3 --> State3["State x3 ... xn-1"]
    State3 --> IntN["Integrator 1/s"]
    IntN --> StateN["State xn"]
    StateN --> Feedback1["Gain -a1"]
    StateN --> Feedback2["Gain -a2"]
    StateN --> FeedbackN["Gain -an"]
    State3 --> FeedbackN
    State2 --> Feedback2
    State1 --> Feedback1
    Feedback1 --> SumNode
    Feedback2 --> SumNode
    FeedbackN --> SumNode
    State1 --> OutGain1["Gain C1"]
    State2 --> OutGain2["Gain C2"]
    StateN --> OutGainN["Gain Cn"]
    OutGain1 --> OutSum["Output Sum"]
    OutGain2 --> OutSum
    OutGainN --> OutSum
    Input --> DirectGain["Gain D = b0"]
    DirectGain --> OutSum
    OutSum --> Output["Output y(t)"]
```

### 4.2 Information Flow Diagram — Matrix Structure to Controllability

This diagram explains the conceptual relationship between the CCF structure and the controllability property.

```mermaid
graph LR
    TransferFn["Transfer Function G(s)"] --> Extract["Extract Coefficients ai and bj"]
    Extract --> MonicCheck{"Monic Denominator?"}
    MonicCheck -->|No| Normalize["Divide by Leading Coefficient"]
    Normalize --> BuildA
    MonicCheck -->|Yes| BuildA["Build Companion Matrix A"]
    BuildA --> BuildB["Set B = 0 ... 0 1 transposed"]
    BuildA --> BuildC["Compute C = Modified Numerator Row"]
    BuildB --> BuildD["Set D = b0"]
    BuildC --> Assemble["Assemble State Space Quadruple A B C D"]
    BuildD --> Assemble
    Assemble --> Verify["Verify Controllability Matrix"]
    Verify --> FullRank{"Rank = n?"}
    FullRank -->|Yes| Result["Controllable Canonical Form Achieved"]
    FullRank -->|No| Error["Re-derive using Different State Choice"]
```

### 4.3 Signal Flow Graph — Phase Variable Realization for a 2nd Order System

For pedagogical clarity, here is a compact signal flow graph for a generic second-order system in phase variable form, which is structurally identical to the controllable canonical form for $n=2$.

```mermaid
graph LR
    UIn["U(s)"] --> Adder1["(+)"]
    Adder1 --> IntA["1/s"]
    IntA --> X1["x1"]
    X1 --> IntB["1/s"]
    IntB --> X2["x2"]
    X2 --> GainA1["Gain -a1"]
    X2 --> GainA2["Gain -a2"]
    X1 --> GainA2
    GainA1 --> Adder1
    GainA2 --> Adder1
    X1 --> GainC1["Gain C1"]
    X2 --> GainC2["Gain C2"]
    GainC1 --> Adder2["(+)"]
    GainC2 --> Adder2
    UIn --> GainD["Gain D = b0"]
    GainD --> Adder2
    Adder2 --> YOut["Y(s)"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Insight:** The key feature of the CCF is that **all feedback signals** that close the loop come from the *outputs of the integrators* (the states) and combine with the input at a single summing junction. This single-point feedback architecture is what gives the Controllability matrix its structurally non-singular form.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define State Space Representation `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
State space representation is a time-domain mathematical model of a linear time-invariant system that uses two matrix equations: the state equation $\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}(t)$ and the output equation $\mathbf{y}(t) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{D}\mathbf{u}(t)$. Here, $\mathbf{x}$ is the state vector, $\mathbf{u}$ is the input vector, $\mathbf{y}$ is the output vector, and $\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B}, \mathbf{C}, \mathbf{D}$ are the system, input, output, and feedforward matrices respectively. Unlike the transfer function, it can handle MIMO systems and is the standard form for modern control design.

---

#### Question 2: What is the Controllable Canonical Form? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The Controllable Canonical Form (CCF) is a specific structure of the state space matrices $(\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B}, \mathbf{C}, \mathbf{D})$ derived directly from the coefficients of the system's transfer function. In this form, the system matrix $\mathbf{A}$ is a companion matrix with $-a_i$ coefficients in the last row, the input matrix is uniquely $\mathbf{B} = [0, 0, \ldots, 0, 1]^T$, and the output matrix $\mathbf{C}$ contains the modified numerator coefficients. The form is called "controllable" because the controllability matrix $\mathcal{C} = [\mathbf{B} \; \mathbf{AB} \; \cdots \; \mathbf{A}^{n-1}\mathbf{B}]$ is guaranteed to have full rank $n$ by construction.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Statement:** For the transfer function of a robotic manipulator joint given by:

$$
G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{s^2 + 3s + 2}{s^3 + 7s^2 + 14s + 8}
$$

**(a)** Derive the Controllable Canonical Form of the state space representation. Show the matrices $\mathbf{A}$, $\mathbf{B}$, $\mathbf{C}$, and $\mathbf{D}$ explicitly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Verify that the controllability matrix $\mathcal{C} = [\mathbf{B} \; \mathbf{AB} \; \mathbf{A}^2\mathbf{B}]$ is of full rank, confirming the system is controllable. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify the coefficients.**

Since the numerator order equals the denominator order, the transfer function is *proper* with $b_0 \neq 0$. The coefficients are:
- Denominator: $a_1 = 7$, $a_2 = 14$, $a_3 = 8$
- Numerator: $b_0 = 1$, $b_1 = 3$, $b_2 = 2$, $b_3 = 0$

**Step 2 — Construct $\mathbf{A}$ (a $3 \times 3$ companion matrix).**

$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1 \\
-a_3 & -a_2 & -a_1
\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1 \\
-8 & -14 & -7
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Construct $\mathbf{B}$ using the canonical form.**

$$
\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Construct $\mathbf{C}$ with modified coefficients.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= b_3 - a_3 b_0 = 0 - (8)(1) = -8 \\
C_2 &= b_2 - a_2 b_0 = 2 - (14)(1) = -12 \\
C_3 &= b_1 - a_1 b_0 = 3 - (7)(1) = -4
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:
$$
\mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} -8 & -12 & -4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Identify $\mathbf{D}$.**

$$
\mathbf{D} = b_0 = 1
$$

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $\mathbf{B}$.**

$$
\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\mathbf{AB}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{AB} &= \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1 \\
-8 & -14 & -7
\end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ -7 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\mathbf{A}^2\mathbf{B} = \mathbf{A}(\mathbf{AB})$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{A}^2\mathbf{B} &= \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1 \\
-8 & -14 & -7
\end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ -7 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -7 \\ 35 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Detailed arithmetic: Row 1: $0(0) + 1(1) + 0(-7) = 1$; Row 2: $0(0) + 0(1) + 1(-7) = -7$; Row 3: $-8(0) + (-14)(1) + (-7)(-7) = 0 - 14 + 49 = 35$)

**Step 4 — Form the controllability matrix and compute its rank.**

$$
\mathcal{C} = [\mathbf{B} \; \mathbf{AB} \; \mathbf{A}^2\mathbf{B}] = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 0 & 1 \\
0 & 1 & -7 \\
1 & -7 & 35
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Computing the determinant of $\mathcal{C}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\det(\mathcal{C}) &= 0 \cdot \det\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -7 \\ -7 & 35 \end{bmatrix} - 0 \cdot \det\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -7 \\ 1 & 35 \end{bmatrix} + 1 \cdot \det\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & -7 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= 1 \cdot (0 \cdot (-7) - 1 \cdot 1) = -1 \neq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\det(\mathcal{C}) = -1 \neq 0$, the matrix $\mathcal{C}$ has full rank equal to 3. The system is therefore completely controllable, verifying the property of the Controllable Canonical Form. $\blacksquare$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Statement:** Consider the electrical network of a robotic actuator modeled by the transfer function:

$$
G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{4s + 12}{s^2 + 5s + 6}
$$

**(a)** Convert this transfer function into the Phase Variable Form of the state space representation. Clearly define the state variables. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the controllability matrix and state whether the system in phase variable form is fully controllable. Justify your answer. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify the coefficients.**

The transfer function is strictly proper (numerator order = 1 < denominator order = 2). Coefficients are:
- $a_1 = 5$, $a_2 = 6$, $b_0 = 0$, $b_1 = 4$, $b_2 = 12$

**Step 2 — Define the state variables as phase variables.**

The phase variables are chosen as the output and its derivatives:

$$
x_1 = y, \quad x_2 = \dot{y}
$$

**Step 3 — Formulate the state equations.**

Differentiating $x_1$: $\dot{x}_1 = x_2$.

For the second equation, we use the input-output differential equation. Cross-multiplying the transfer function:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(s^2 + 5s + 6) Y(s) &= (4s + 12) U(s) \\
\ddot{y} + 5\dot{y} + 6y &= 4\dot{u} + 12u
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $b_0 = 0$, the system is strictly proper, and the input derivatives vanish from the state equations. We have $\ddot{y} = -5\dot{y} - 6y + 4\dot{u} + 12u$. However, to keep the phase variable form standard, we must avoid input derivatives. We therefore introduce an alternative state equation by choosing a different intermediate variable.

**Alternative approach using the canonical form for strictly proper systems:**

For a strictly proper transfer function, the phase variable form is identical to the controllable canonical form. The matrices are:

$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the output matrix $\mathbf{C}$.**

For strictly proper systems, $b_0 = 0$, so $\mathbf{C}$ contains only the original numerator coefficients:
$$
\mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} b_2 - a_2 b_0 & b_1 - a_1 b_0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 12 & 4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

And $\mathbf{D} = b_0 = 0$.

**Step 5 — State the final phase variable form.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{\mathbf{x}} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{x} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u \\
y &= \begin{bmatrix} 12 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{x}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $\mathbf{B}$.**

$$
\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\mathbf{AB}$.**

$$
\mathbf{AB} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the controllability matrix.**

$$
\mathcal{C} = [\mathbf{B} \; \mathbf{AB}] = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & -5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the determinant and rank.**

$$
\det(\mathcal{C}) = (0)(-5) - (1)(1) = -1 \neq 0
$$

Since the determinant is non-zero, the $2 \times 2$ controllability matrix has rank 2, which is the full system order. The system is **completely controllable** in phase variable form.

**Justification:** This confirms the structural property of the companion-form-based representations (Phase Variable Form and Controllable Canonical Form) — when the transfer function coefficients $a_i$ are real and the companion matrix is non-degenerate, the controllability matrix is automatically non-singular. The system is therefore state-controllable from the single input $u$. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Last Row of $\mathbf{A}$:** Students frequently place $+a_n$ instead of $-a_n$ in the last row of the system matrix. The correct convention uses **negative** coefficients. This single sign error cascades to every subsequent calculation.
> 2. **Modified vs. Unmodified $\mathbf{C}$:** When the transfer function is *proper* (not strictly proper), students must use the **modified** formula $C_i = b_{n+1-i} - a_{n+1-i} b_0$. Using the unmodified numerator coefficients directly is a common error that loses 3-4 marks.
> 3. **Order of Coefficients in $\mathbf{C}$:** The first entry of $\mathbf{C}$ corresponds to the **lowest** state $x_1$ (not $x_n$). The coefficient ordering is **reversed** relative to the polynomial — a classic confusion point.
> 4. **Confusing PVF and CCF:** Many students write $\mathbf{B} = [0, 0, 1]^T$ and call it "Phase Variable Form." This is the Controllable Canonical Form. The Phase Variable Form has an **arbitrary** $\mathbf{B}$ that depends on the input coefficients.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Matrix Representation of State Equations**

- **State Equation** is always $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}$; **Output Equation** is always $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{D}\mathbf{u}$.
- The **Monic Denominator Convention** is mandatory: the coefficient of $s^n$ must equal 1 before applying the CCF formulas.
- The **System Matrix $\mathbf{A}$** in CCF is a companion matrix: upper superdiagonal contains 1s, last row contains $[-a_n, -a_{n-1}, \ldots, -a_1]$, and all other entries are 0.
- The **Input Matrix $\mathbf{B}$** in CCF is *uniquely* $[0, 0, \ldots, 0, 1]^T$ — this is the single most defining feature of the form.
- The **Output Matrix $\mathbf{C}$** entries are computed as $C_i = b_{n+1-i} - a_{n+1-i} b_0$ (modified coefficients).
- The **Feedforward Matrix $\mathbf{D} = b_0$** captures the direct feedthrough from input to output.
- For a **Strictly Proper** system ($b_0 = 0$): $\mathbf{D} = 0$ and $\mathbf{C} = [b_n, b_{n-1}, \ldots, b_1]$ (unmodified).
- The **Controllability Matrix** $\mathcal{C} = [\mathbf{B} \; \mathbf{AB} \; \mathbf{A}^2\mathbf{B} \; \cdots \; \mathbf{A}^{n-1}\mathbf{B}]$ is always full rank in CCF, guaranteeing state controllability.
- The **Phase Variable Form** uses $x_i = y^{(i-1)}$ as state definitions and produces a companion matrix $\mathbf{A}$ identical to CCF, but the input matrix $\mathbf{B}$ is *not* constrained to $[0, \ldots, 0, 1]^T$.
- **Conversion verification** can be done by computing $\det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})$ and confirming it matches the denominator of $G(s)$.
- The CCF is the **canonical form of choice** for state-feedback pole placement (Ackermann's formula) and LQR design in robotic control systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Observable
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT601 - ROBOTIC CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Observable

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the rigorous framework of **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) state-space control theory**, the term **"Observable"** refers to a fundamental structural property of a dynamical system that quantifies our ability to reconstruct the complete internal state vector $\mathbf{x}(t)$ of the system by observing only its measurable output $\mathbf{y}(t)$ and known input $\mathbf{u}(t)$ over a finite time interval.

Consider a standard LTI state-space system governed by:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}(t)
$$

$$
\mathbf{y}(t) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{D}\mathbf{u}(t)
$$

where $\mathbf{x}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the state vector, $\mathbf{u}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the input vector, $\mathbf{y}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ is the output vector, and $\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B}, \mathbf{C}, \mathbf{D}$ are constant matrices of appropriate dimensions.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition of Observability**
> 
> A system is said to be **completely observable** if, for any unknown initial state $\mathbf{x}(t_0)$, there exists a finite time $t_1 > t_0$ such that the knowledge of the input $\mathbf{u}(t)$ and output $\mathbf{y}(t)$ over the interval $[t_0, t_1]$ is sufficient to uniquely determine $\mathbf{x}(t_0)$. If such reconstruction is impossible, the system is termed **unobservable** or only **partially observable**.

The companion concept is **Controllability** (the ability to drive the state to any desired point using inputs), and together these form the famous **Kalman Decomposition** of linear systems.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The Medical Diagnosis Paradigm

> [!NOTE] **Conceptual Analogy: The Doctor and the Black-Box Patient**
> 
> Imagine a patient walks into a clinic. The doctor cannot directly "see" the internal state of the patient's body (organ health, blood chemistry, neural activity). However, the doctor can measure **outputs** through external instruments: blood pressure, temperature, ECG readings, X-ray images, and verbal symptoms.
> 
> - The **patient's internal state** = the **state vector** $\mathbf{x}(t)$ of the system.
> - The **medical tests and observations** = the **output measurements** $\mathbf{y}(t)$.
> - The **prescribed medicines (inputs)** = the **control input** $\mathbf{u}(t)$.
> - **Observability** = Can the doctor accurately diagnose the full internal condition of the patient from a finite set of tests?
> 
> If the doctor can fully diagnose the patient from external tests alone, the patient is **completely observable**. If some hidden condition cannot be detected by any test, that aspect is **unobservable** — just like an unobservable mode in a control system.

### Extension to Robotics
In a **robotic manipulator**, the joint positions and velocities form the state vector. Sensors such as **encoders, gyroscopes, and cameras** provide output measurements. The robot is **observable** if, from these sensor readings, we can uniquely reconstruct the entire state (including unmeasured variables like link velocities or contact forces). This is critical for **state feedback control** and **sensorless estimation**.

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Mathematical Constants

The following critical quantities characterize observability:

| Metric | Symbol | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Order of the system | $n$ | Number of state variables |
| Observability Matrix | $\mathcal{O}$ | Encodes the mapping from states to outputs |
| Observability Index | $\nu$ | Smallest integer such that $\text{rank}(\mathcal{O}_\nu) = n$ |
| Observability Gramian | $\mathbf{W}_o$ | Energy-based measure of observability |
| Condition Number | $\kappa(\mathcal{O})$ | Robustness of observability |

> [!IMPORTANT] The **observability index** $\nu$ is bounded by: $\frac{n}{p} \leq \nu \leq n - p + 1$, where $p$ is the number of outputs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Formulation of the Observability Problem

The fundamental question of observability is: **Given $\mathbf{u}(t)$ and $\mathbf{y}(t)$ for $t \in [t_0, t_1]$, can we uniquely solve for $\mathbf{x}(t_0)$?**

Starting from the state equation solution (assuming zero initial time $t_0 = 0$ for simplicity):

$$
\mathbf{x}(t) = e^{\mathbf{A}t}\mathbf{x}(0) + \int_{0}^{t} e^{\mathbf{A}(t-\tau)}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

The measured output becomes:

$$
\mathbf{y}(t) = \mathbf{C}\left[e^{\mathbf{A}t}\mathbf{x}(0) + \int_{0}^{t} e^{\mathbf{A}(t-\tau)}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}(\tau)\, d\tau\right] + \mathbf{D}\mathbf{u}(t)
$$

Since the input history $\mathbf{u}(t)$ is known, the integral term and the $\mathbf{D}\mathbf{u}(t)$ term can be subtracted, leaving a function of $\mathbf{x}(0)$ alone. The observability question therefore reduces to:

> **Can we uniquely determine $\mathbf{x}(0)$ from the function $\mathbf{C} e^{\mathbf{A}t}\mathbf{x}(0)$ observed over a finite time interval?**

## 2.2 Derivation of the Observability Matrix (Kalman's Test)

Repeated differentiation of the output (assuming $\mathbf{D} = \mathbf{0}$ for clarity) yields:

$$
\mathbf{y}(0) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x}(0)
$$

$$
\dot{\mathbf{y}}(0) = \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{x}}(0) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}(0) + \mathbf{C}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}(0)
$$

Ignoring the known input term, and continuing this process $n$ times:

$$
\mathbf{y}^{(k)}(0) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{A}^{k}\mathbf{x}(0) + \text{(known input terms)}
$$

Stacking these $n$ equations into a single matrix equation:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{y}(0) \\ \dot{\mathbf{y}}(0) \\ \ddot{\mathbf{y}}(0) \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{y}^{(n-1)}(0) \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{C} \\ \mathbf{C}\mathbf{A} \\ \mathbf{C}\mathbf{A}^{2} \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{C}\mathbf{A}^{n-1} \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{x}(0) + \text{(known input contributions)}
$$

For $\mathbf{x}(0)$ to have a **unique** solution, the stacked matrix on the right must have **full column rank** $n$. This stacked matrix is the **Observability Matrix** $\mathcal{O}$.

## 2.3 Kalman's Observability Test (Canonical KTU Statement)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Kalman's Observability Criterion (KCU Standard)**
> 
> An $n$-th order LTI system $(\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{C})$ is **completely observable** if and only if the **observability matrix** $\mathcal{O} \in \mathbb{R}^{np \times n}$ defined as:
> $$\mathcal{O} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{C} \\ \mathbf{C}\mathbf{A} \\ \mathbf{C}\mathbf{A}^{2} \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{C}\mathbf{A}^{n-1} \end{bmatrix}$$
> has **full rank**, i.e., $\text{rank}(\mathcal{O}) = n$.

Equivalently, the determinant of the square Gramian-like product $\mathcal{O}^{T}\mathcal{O}$ must be non-zero:

$$
\det(\mathcal{O}^{T}\mathcal{O}) \neq 0
$$

## 2.4 Observability Gramian (Infinite-Horizon, Energy-Based View)

For stable systems, observability can also be characterized through the **observability gramian** $\mathbf{W}_o$, which measures the energy that flows from initial states to outputs:

$$
\mathbf{W}_o = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{\mathbf{A}^{T}t}\mathbf{C}^{T}\mathbf{C}e^{\mathbf{A}t}\, dt
$$

This gramian satisfies the continuous-time **Lyapunov equation**:

$$
\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}_o + \mathbf{W}_o\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{C}^{T}\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{0}
$$

The system is observable if and only if $\mathbf{W}_o$ is **positive definite**, i.e., $\mathbf{W}_o \succ 0$.

## 2.5 The Principle of Duality (Controllability ↔ Observability)

> [!NOTE] **Kalman's Duality Theorem**
> 
> The system $(\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B}, \mathbf{C})$ is **observable** if and only if its **dual system** $(\mathbf{A}^{T}, \mathbf{C}^{T}, \mathbf{B}^{T})$ is **controllable**.

This powerful theorem allows us to convert every observability problem into a controllability problem by simply transposing the system matrices. In practice, it means that the **observability matrix** of $(\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{C})$ has the same rank as the **controllability matrix** of $(\mathbf{A}^{T}, \mathbf{C}^{T})$.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **All formulas required for KTU 2024 ESE on Observability**

| **#** | **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **Condition / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LTI State-Space Form | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}, \;\; \mathbf{y} = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{D}\mathbf{u}$ | Standard canonical form |
| 2 | Observability Matrix $\mathcal{O}$ | $\mathcal{O} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{C} \\ \mathbf{C}\mathbf{A} \\ \mathbf{C}\mathbf{A}^{2} \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{C}\mathbf{A}^{n-1} \end{bmatrix}$ | Dimensions: $np \times n$ |
| 3 | Kalman's Observability Test | $\text{rank}(\mathcal{O}) = n$ | Necessary and sufficient |
| 4 | Determinant Test (Square Case) | $\det(\mathcal{O}^{T}\mathcal{O}) \neq 0$ | For checking non-singularity |
| 5 | Observability Gramian (CT) | $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}_o + \mathbf{W}_o\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{C}^{T}\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{0}$ | Stable systems only |
| 6 | Observability Gramian (DT) | $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{W}_o\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{W}_o + \mathbf{C}^{T}\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{0}$ | Discrete-time Lyapunov |
| 7 | Duality Theorem | $(\mathbf{A},\mathbf{C})$ observable $\iff$ $(\mathbf{A}^{T},\mathbf{C}^{T})$ controllable | Direct correspondence |
| 8 | PBH Test (Popov-Belevitch-Hautus) | $\text{rank}\begin{bmatrix} \lambda \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A} \\ \mathbf{C} \end{bmatrix} = n \;\; \forall \lambda \in \sigma(\mathbf{A})$ | Frequency-domain alternative |
| 9 | Unobservable Modes | Eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ for which PBH rank fails | Cause hidden dynamics |
| 10 | Observer Error Dynamics | $\dot{\mathbf{e}} = (\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{L}\mathbf{C})\mathbf{e}$ | Luenberger observer |
| 11 | Observer Design (Pole Placement) | $\det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A} + \mathbf{L}\mathbf{C}) = \text{desired char. poly.}$ | Choose $\mathbf{L}$ via Ackermann's formula |
| 12 | Ackermann's Observer Formula | $\mathbf{L} = \alpha_c(\mathbf{A}) \cdot (\mathcal{O}^{T}\mathcal{O})^{-1}\mathcal{O}^{T}\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{0}\\\vdots\\\mathbf{I}_p\end{bmatrix}$ | Single-output systems |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE] **Where Observability Matters in Robotics**
> 
> 1. **Sensor Placement in Robot Manipulators**: Where to mount encoders, force/torque sensors, IMUs to make the robot state fully observable.
> 2. **State Estimation (Kalman Filter)**: The Kalman filter is essentially a stochastic observer; observability guarantees convergence.
> 3. **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)**: A robot's pose and map must be observable from lidar/camera measurements.
> 4. **Fault Detection**: An unobservable fault cannot be detected from outputs.
> 5. **MEMS and Drone Control**: Inertial measurement units must be paired with GPS or vision for full state observability.
> 6. **Sensorless Motor Control**: Back-EMF-based rotor position estimation requires observability of flux linkages.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: From Output Derivatives to the Observability Matrix

We start with the state-space system (assuming $\mathbf{D} = \mathbf{0}$ and $\mathbf{u}(t) = 0$ for an unforced analysis):

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}(t)
$$

$$
\mathbf{y}(t) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x}(t)
$$

**Step 1: Write the output at $t = 0$.**
The output at the initial time directly gives:

$$
\mathbf{y}(0) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x}(0) \quad \text{--- (Equation 1)}
$$

**Step 2: Differentiate the output to get $\dot{\mathbf{y}}(0)$.**
Differentiating $\mathbf{y}(t) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x}(t)$ and substituting the state equation:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{y}}(t) = \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}(t)
$$

Evaluating at $t = 0$:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{y}}(0) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}(0) \quad \text{--- (Equation 2)}
$$

**Step 3: Differentiate again to get $\ddot{\mathbf{y}}(0)$.**

$$
\ddot{\mathbf{y}}(t) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{A}\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{A}^{2}\mathbf{x}(t)
$$

Thus:

$$
\ddot{\mathbf{y}}(0) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{A}^{2}\mathbf{x}(0) \quad \text{--- (Equation 3)}
$$

**Step 4: General $k$-th derivative.**

By induction, the $k$-th derivative of the output is:

$$
\mathbf{y}^{(k)}(0) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{A}^{k}\mathbf{x}(0)
$$

**Step 5: Stacking into a single system.**

Stacking equations (1) through ($n-1$) into a single matrix equation:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{y}(0) \\ \dot{\mathbf{y}}(0) \\ \ddot{\mathbf{y}}(0) \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{y}^{(n-1)}(0) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{C} \\ \mathbf{C}\mathbf{A} \\ \mathbf{C}\mathbf{A}^{2} \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{C}\mathbf{A}^{n-1} \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{x}(0) = \mathcal{O} \cdot \mathbf{x}(0)
$$

**Step 6: Uniqueness argument.**
The vector $\mathbf{x}(0)$ is uniquely determined if and only if the matrix $\mathcal{O}$ has linearly independent columns, i.e., $\text{rank}(\mathcal{O}) = n$. This proves **Kalman's Observability Criterion**.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Observability Test for a 3rd-Order Robotic Joint System

Consider a state-space model of a single robotic joint:

$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1: Construct the first row of $\mathcal{O}$.**

$$
\mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $\mathbf{C}\mathbf{A}$.**

$$
\mathbf{C}\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Compute $\mathbf{C}\mathbf{A}^{2} = (\mathbf{C}\mathbf{A})\mathbf{A}$.**

$$
\mathbf{C}\mathbf{A}^{2} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: Assemble the observability matrix.**

$$
\mathcal{O} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{C} \\ \mathbf{C}\mathbf{A} \\ \mathbf{C}\mathbf{A}^{2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{I}_{3}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the rank.**

$$
\text{rank}(\mathcal{O}) = \text{rank}(\mathbf{I}_{3}) = 3 = n
$$

> [!NOTE] **Conclusion**: Since $\text{rank}(\mathcal{O}) = 3 = n$, the system is **completely observable**. In this special case, the observability matrix is the identity matrix, making it trivially invertible.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Luenberger Observer

When the system is observable, we can build an **observer** (a software/hardware estimator) that reconstructs the state from the output. The classical **Luenberger full-order observer** is:

$$
\dot{\hat{\mathbf{x}}}(t) = \mathbf{A}\hat{\mathbf{x}}(t) + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}(t) + \mathbf{L}\left[\mathbf{y}(t) - \mathbf{C}\hat{\mathbf{x}}(t)\right]
$$

where $\hat{\mathbf{x}}(t)$ is the estimated state and $\mathbf{L}$ is the **observer gain matrix**.

**Step 1: Define the estimation error.**

$$
\mathbf{e}(t) = \mathbf{x}(t) - \hat{\mathbf{x}}(t)
$$

**Step 2: Derive the error dynamics.**

Differentiating the error:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{e}}(t) = \dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) - \dot{\hat{\mathbf{x}}}(t)
$$

Substituting both dynamics:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{e}}(t) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u} - \left[\mathbf{A}\hat{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{L}(\mathbf{C}\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{C}\hat{\mathbf{x}})\right]
$$

The $\mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}$ terms cancel, leaving:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{e}}(t) = (\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{L}\mathbf{C})\mathbf{e}(t)
$$

**Step 3: Stability condition.**

The estimation error converges to zero ($\mathbf{e}(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$) if and only if the eigenvalues of $(\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{L}\mathbf{C})$ all lie in the **left half-plane** (LHP) for continuous-time systems, i.e.:

$$
\text{Re}(\lambda_i(\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{L}\mathbf{C})) < 0 \quad \forall i
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Observer Design via Duality**
> 
> For single-output systems, the observer gain is found by **Ackermann's formula** applied to the dual system:
> $$\mathbf{L} = \alpha_c(\mathbf{A}) \cdot \mathcal{O}^{-1}\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{0}\\\vdots\\\mathbf{0}\\1\end{bmatrix}$$
> where $\alpha_c(s)$ is the desired characteristic polynomial with the chosen observer poles.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Luenberger Observer Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how a Luenberger observer processes inputs and outputs to estimate the internal state of a plant.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    refIN[Reference Input u t] --> plantA
    plantA[Plant Dynamics A B C D]
    plantA --> realOUT[Measured Output y t]
    plantA -.unknown.-> realState[True State x t]

    refIN --> obsA
    obsA[Observer Dynamics A B L C]
    obsA --> estState[Estimated State xhat t]

    realOUT --> sumDiff
    estState --> multC
    multC[Output Estimate C xhat] --> sumDiff
    sumDiff[Subtractor y minus C xhat] --> gainL
    gainL[Observer Gain Matrix L] --> obsA

    estState --> ctrlA[State Feedback Controller K]
    realState -. feedback conceptual.-> ctrlA

    classDef extIO fill:#E8F1FF,stroke:#0B5FB3,stroke-width:1px,color:#0B2F66
    classDef plantBox fill:#FFE9C9,stroke:#B36B00,stroke-width:1px,color:#5A2E00
    classDef obsBox fill:#DDF5DD,stroke:#1F7A1F,stroke-width:1px,color:#0B3D0B

    class refIN,realOUT,estState,realState extIO
    class plantA,multC,sumDiff,ctrlA plantBox
    class obsA,gainL obsBox
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the Diagram**: The plant produces the true output $\mathbf{y}(t)$ from input $\mathbf{u}(t)$ and unknown state $\mathbf{x}(t)$. The observer computes a correction term $\mathbf{L}[\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{C}\hat{\mathbf{x}}]$ that drives the estimation error to zero, ensuring $\hat{\mathbf{x}}(t) \to \mathbf{x}(t)$.

## 4.2 Procedural Flowchart — Kalman's Observability Test

This flowchart captures the algorithmic decision procedure every KTU student should memorize for the examination.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start Observability Test]) --> getSys[Obtain Matrices A and C]
    getSys --> orderCheck{Is n known?}
    orderCheck -- Yes --> buildRows[Build Rows C, CA, CA2, ..., CA power n-1]
    orderCheck -- No --> findOrder[Find system order n]
    findOrder --> buildRows
    buildRows --> stackM[Stack Rows into O Matrix]
    stackM --> compRank[Compute rank of O]
    compRank --> compare{rank O equals n?}
    compare -- Yes --> obsYes[System is Completely Observable]
    compare -- No --> obsNo[System is NOT Completely Observable]
    obsNo --> findUnobs[Identify Unobservable Eigenvalues via PBH Test]
    findUnobs --> reportUnobs[Report Modes Lying Outside Observable Subspace]
    obsYes --> endA([End])
    reportUnobs --> endA

    classDef startEnd fill:#1F4E79,stroke:#0B2F4F,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef processBox fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#B58900,stroke-width:1px,color:#5A4400
    classDef decisionBox fill:#F8D7DA,stroke:#9B1C20,stroke-width:1.2px,color:#5A0A0E
    classDef resultBox fill:#D1E7DD,stroke:#0F5132,stroke-width:1.2px,color:#062B19

    class startA,endA startEnd
    class getSys,buildRows,stackM,compRank,findUnobs,reportUnobs,findingOrder processBox
    class orderCheck,compare decisionBox
    class obsYes,obsNo resultBox
```

## 4.3 Concept Map — Observability and its Related Concepts

```mermaid

    sysProp[System Structural Properties] --> contr[Controllability]
    sysProp --> obs[Observability]

    sysProp --> reach[Reachability]

    obs --> kalmanT[Kalman Test Matrix Rank equals n]
    obs --> pbhT[PBH Frequency Test]

    obs --> app1[Luenberger Observer]

    obs --> app4[Fault Detection and Diagnosis]

    kalmanT --> defO[Definition of O Matrix]
    pbhT --> defO
    gramT --> defW[Definition of W o]

    classDef rootNode fill:#1F4E79,stroke:#0B2F4F,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef coreNode fill:#D6EAF8,stroke:#1B4F72,stroke-width:1px,color:#0B2F4F
```

> [!NOTE] **Tip for Visual Learners**: The concept map shows that observability is one of four canonical structural properties, and links observability to its three mathematical tests and four real-world robotic applications. This is exactly the kind of "broad picture" examiners often probe in **Part A (3-mark)** questions.
